ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Schezwan Prawn. FOODON_03302051 (shrimp food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific Szechuan prawn dish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955003 (Prawn) is the closest available substance concept. No prepared Schezwan Prawn dish concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Schezwan Prawn is approximately 250 g, comprising 8–12 medium-to-large prawns coated in spiced Szechuan sauce, served as a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152) adapted with Indo-Chinese Szechuan sauce composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base prawn macros derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180). Sauce contribution (chilli, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, oil) estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and ICMR-NIN Indo-Chinese dish analogues. Energy, fat, sodium, and carbohydrate adjusted upward to reflect sauce and oil content typical of restaurant-style Schezwan preparation. Values represent a plausible composite per-100g estimate for the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate from mixed seafood and low-carbohydrate spiced dish category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Schezwan Prawn. GI estimated at 35 based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein profile of cooked prawns (GI effectively low per University of Sydney GI Database for seafood dishes) combined with the modest carbohydrate contribution of Szechuan sauce (chilli, soy, vinegar base). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 5.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.1 g. This is consistent with low-GI, moderate-GL classification for a protein-dominant seafood dish.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and Szechuan sauce base
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a 250 g serving provides approximately 1300 mg sodium — over half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content from prawns
type2_diabetes.guidance: Schezwan Prawn has a low estimated GI of 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 5 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While prawns provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, the high sodium content of the Szechuan sauce and the moderate cholesterol level (~370 mg per serving) warrant caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; portion control and low-sodium sauce variants are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Schezwan Prawn is an Indo-Chinese fusion dish popular in Indian restaurants and food delivery platforms across Singapore and India. It consists of medium-to-large prawns stir-fried or sautéed in a fiery Szechuan-style sauce made from dried red chillies, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, vinegar, and chilli oil, often finished with spring onions. The dish delivers a bold, spicy, tangy, and umami-rich flavour profile with a slightly sticky sauce coating the prawns. The texture contrasts the firm, succulent bite of the prawn with the aromatic heat of the sauce. Nutritionally, Schezwan Prawn is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with a low glycemic index (~35), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management. However, the sodium content is high due to soy sauce and chilli sauce components, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 142 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 7.2 g fat, and 5.8 g carbohydrate. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice, fried rice, or naan in Indian-Chinese restaurant settings. The dish is halal-friendly and dairy-free but contains crustacean and soy allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Schezwan Prawn?
Is Schezwan Prawn good for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of Szechuan Prawn?
What allergens are in Schezwan Prawn?

Can I eat Schezwan Prawn if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Schezwan Prawn found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data with estimated sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database seafood category. Confidence is medium rather than low because the prawn base is well-characterised and the sauce composition is standard for this dish class.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for cooked shrimp and is not reliably estimated for this sauce-based preparation. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible for stir-fried prawn dishes using vegetable oil per USDA SR Legacy data. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the finished restaurant-style dish per 100 g. Sodium is notably high due to soy sauce and Szechuan sauce components; individuals with hypertension should exercise caution. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for Schezwan Prawn exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (35 × 5.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.1 g. The cuisine tag 'Naan' in the input record is not applicable to this dish and has been disregarded. This dish is classified as Indo-Chinese fusion under the Indian cuisine umbrella. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine compliance: Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.
